content:
9 min read
Just now

--

The Open Knowledge Format is the standard that makes agent memory portable, versioned, and readable by every tool you use.

Every developer using an AI coding agent has hit the same wall. The session ends. The next morning, you start fresh and the agent has forgotten everything. It re-discovers your auth flow. It re-guesses the reason behind a three-year-old architectural decision. It reads the same five files again to rebuild context you spent an hour explaining yesterday.
The standard fix is CLAUDE.md or AGENTS.md — a context file that loads at the start of every session. But these are blunt instruments. They load everything, every time, whether or not it’s relevant. They’re written for one agent on one platform and don’t travel well. They mix behavioral instructions (“always write tests”) with factual knowledge (“the orders table joins to customers on customer_id”) in ways that get messy fast.
On June 12, 2026, Google Cloud published a specification that changes this. It’s called the Open Knowledge Format, and it’s the cleanest solution to the agent memory problem that’s appeared yet. Here’s what it is, how it works, and why it matters more than most people realize.

The Problem It Solves
Before getting into what OKF is, it helps to understand exactly what it’s fixing.
When an agent needs to answer “how do we compute weekly active users from our event stream?”, the answer is scattered across at least four places:
+-----------------------+--------------------------------------------+
| WHERE IT LIVES | WHAT'S THERE |
+-----------------------+--------------------------------------------+
| Metadata catalogs | Table schemas (but vendor-locked APIs) |
| Wiki / Notion | Runbooks, metric definitions |
| Code comments | Docstrings, inline notes |
| People's heads | Join paths, deprecation warnings |
+-----------------------+--------------------------------------------+None of it travels with the code. None of it survives a fresh context window. Every agent builder re-solves the same context assembly problem from scratch. Every catalog vendor reinvents schemas. The knowledge is locked inside whatever surface created it.
OKF’s bet is that the missing piece is a format, not another platform.

What OKF Actually Is
Open Knowledge Format v0.1 is a vendor-neutral, open specification (Apache 2.0) for representing knowledge as a directory of markdown files with YAML frontmatter. That description sounds simple. It is simple. That’s the point.
No schema registry. No runtime engine. No SDK required. If you can cat a file, you can read it. If you can git clone a repo, you can ship it.
The entire spec fits on one page. It has exactly one required field. Everything else is optional or producer-defined.

The Structure: A Directory Is a Knowledge Graph
An OKF bundle is just a folder. File path = concept identity.
.okf/
+-- index.md <- progressive entry point
+-- log.md <- dated change history, newest first
+-- services/
| +-- index.md
| +-- auth-api.md <- one concept = one file
| +-- payments-service.md
+-- datasets/
| +-- index.md
| +-- orders-db.md
+-- metrics/
| +-- index.md
| +-- weekly-active-users.md
+-- decisions/
+-- index.md
+-- why-we-use-postgres.mdTwo files are reserved across every bundle:

• index.md - progressive disclosure. Agents navigate the bundle by reading index files at each level, drilling down only when the task is relevant. This is the same progressive loading that makes Claude Skills token-efficient.

• log.md - ISO-dated change history, newest first. Human and agent maintainers both update it when concepts change.
Every other file is a concept document. The path is its ID.

Anatomy of an OKF Concept File
Each concept needs exactly one thing in its frontmatter: a non-empty type field. Everything else is optional.
---
type: Service
title: "Auth API"
description: "Issues and verifies short-lived access tokens."
resource: https://github.com/acme/auth
tags: [auth, platform]
timestamp: 2026-06-14T10:00:00Z
---## Endpoints| Method | Path | Description |
|--------|----------|---------------------------|
| POST | /token | Exchange creds for a JWT. |
| GET | /verify | Validate a token. |## Why This ExistsSee the decision at [decisions/why-we-separated-auth.md](../decisions/why-we-separated-auth.md).
Joins with [datasets/orders-db.md](../datasets/orders-db.md) for user scoping.That last part is the key design move. Concepts link to each other with standard markdown links. The directory becomes a graph. Not a flat list of documents — a graph of typed, cross-referenced knowledge where the relationships are explicit and traversable.
[auth-api.md]
/ \
links links
/ \
[decisions/why-separated-auth.md] [datasets/orders-db.md]
|
links
|
[datasets/customers-db.md]Any tool that can follow a markdown link can traverse this graph. Any agent with file access can read and write it.

The Three Design Principles
1. Minimally opinionated
Only type is required. What types exist (Service, Dataset, Metric, Decision, Runbook), what optional fields to use, what body sections to include - those are the producer's choice. OKF defines interoperability, not content models.
2. Producer and consumer independence
PRODUCERS CONSUMERS
--------- ---------
Human authors ----> AI agents
BigQuery pipelines ----> HTML visualizers
LLM-generated ----> Search indexes
Wiki exports ----> Other agents / toolsThe same format works at both ends. Swapping producers or consumers doesn’t break the knowledge.
3. Format, not platform
No proprietary account. No SDK. No central registry. Value scales with adoption breadth, not vendor lock-in. This is the same design philosophy as git, markdown, and JSON — the formats that lasted because they required nothing to use.

OKF vs Everything Else You’re Already Using
This is where most people get confused. OKF doesn’t replace what you’re already using. It fills a specific gap.
+---------------------+---------------------------+---------------------------+
| TOOL | PURPOSE | SCOPE |
+---------------------+---------------------------+---------------------------+
| CLAUDE.md | How the agent behaves | Per-project, one agent |
| AGENTS.md | Repo instructions | Per-repo, tool-specific |
| Karpathy LLM wiki | Agent-maintained notes | Pattern, not a spec |
| MCP | Live tool access | Runtime connections |
| OKF | What the team knows | Cross-project, any agent |
+---------------------+---------------------------+---------------------------+The cleanest mental model:

• CLAUDE.md answers: how should the agent behave?

• OKF answers: what does the team know about this system?

• MCP answers: what live tools can the agent call right now?
They stack. A CLAUDE.md file might say: “Before any analytics task, read .okf/metrics/." The OKF bundle is the structured knowledge CLAUDE.md points at. MCP connections are the live data sources OKF documents describe.

The Karpathy Connection
Andrej Karpathy (who coined “vibe coding” and now calls it “agentic engineering”) published an LLM wiki gist that attracted 5,000+ stars with a simple argument: humans abandon wikis when bookkeeping becomes a burden. Agents don’t. An LLM doesn’t get bored, doesn’t forget to update a cross-reference, and can touch 15 files in one pass.
The pattern kept appearing independently — Obsidian + coding agents, AGENTS.md repos, index.md/log.md artifacts in dozens of projects. People were reinventing the same thing.
OKF’s contribution is agreeing on what fields every document carries and what filenames mean — so your wiki and my wiki and a BigQuery catalog export can cooperate without translation.
The three-layer mapping:
KARPATHY LAYER OKF EQUIVALENT
-------------- --------------
Raw sources (immutable) --> External datasets, docs, APIs
(OKF bundles are the compiled layer)Wiki (LLM-maintained) --> OKF bundle (*.md + frontmatter)
(OKF adds type, resource, tags, timestamp)Schema (CLAUDE.md) --> Producer/consumer conventions + okf/SPEC.md
(org-wide spec replaces per-vault bespoke rules)Both Karpathy’s gist and OKF reserve index.md and log.md for the same purposes. The convergence wasn't accidental.

What Google Actually Shipped
OKF is a spec, but Google shipped three concrete things alongside it:
1. A BigQuery enrichment agent — walks a BigQuery dataset, drafts OKF concept docs for every table and view, then runs a second LLM pass to enrich with citations, schemas, and join paths from documentation. Point it at your data warehouse; get a populated .okf/ directory.
2. A static HTML visualizer — turns any OKF bundle into an interactive graph view. Single self-contained HTML file. No backend. Nothing leaves the page. Concepts are nodes; markdown links are edges; clicking a node opens a wiki-style detail panel with rendered markdown and backlinks (“Links to / Cited by”).
3. Sample bundles — three real OKF bundles on GitHub using public BigQuery datasets:
+--------------------+----------------------------------+
| BUNDLE | WHAT IT DOCUMENTS |
+--------------------+----------------------------------+
| GA4 e-commerce | Analytics tables and metrics |
| Stack Overflow | Public dataset concepts |
| Bitcoin | Blockchain dataset structure |
+--------------------+----------------------------------+These serve double duty: starter templates to copy, and demonstration that the format works across different domains.

When to Use OKF
OKF is the right choice when your knowledge problem looks like any of these:
You re-explain the same things to every new agent session. If you find yourself pasting the same context block into CLAUDE.md over and over, that context belongs in OKF.
Your knowledge spans multiple projects or teams. CLAUDE.md is per-project and per-agent. OKF bundles are portable across repos, agents, and tools.
You want your project docs to be versionable. OKF lives in git next to the code it describes. Every change is diffable. Every PR can include knowledge updates alongside code changes.
You’re building on Google Cloud / BigQuery. OKF integrates directly with Google’s Knowledge Catalog, which ingests bundles and serves them to GCP agents.
You want a knowledge graph, not a flat wiki. The markdown link graph gives you typed relationships between concepts without a graph database.
OKF is not the right choice when:

• You need real-time data (use MCP instead)

• You need to encode behavioral rules (use CLAUDE.md)

• Your project is small and single-session (CLAUDE.md is enough)

How to Get Started
The setup is minimal by design.
If you use Claude Code:
Install the okf-skills plugin, which ships with three skills: /okf:okf to produce and maintain bundles, /okf:validate for conformance checks, and /okf:visualize to generate the interactive graph.
/plugin marketplace add scaccogatto/okf-skills
/plugin install okf@scaccogattoOr via npm for any agent (Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, and 20+ others):
npx skills add scaccogatto/okf-skillsThen:
/okf:okf produce .okf # create your first bundle
/okf:validate .okf --strict # check conformance
/okf:visualize .okf # generate viz.htmlTo turn on automatic upkeep — where Claude consults .okf/ before tasks and writes knowledge back after changes - paste the templates/CLAUDE-okf.md template into your project's CLAUDE.md. This is entirely opt-in.
If you want to write OKF manually:
Create a .okf/ directory. Add an index.md. Create one concept file per important thing your system does:
---
type: Decision
title: "Why we use Postgres over MySQL"
description: "JSONB support and row-level security were decisive."
timestamp: 2026-03-01T00:00:00Z
tags: [infrastructure, database]
---## ContextIn early 2026 we evaluated both options. MySQL's JSON support was insufficient for our metadata schema...## DecisionPostgres 16. See [datasets/primary-db.md](../datasets/primary-db.md) for schema docs.That’s it. The file is immediately readable by any agent with file access, reviewable in a PR, diffable in git.

The Layered Memory Stack
The full picture of how OKF fits alongside everything else:
+------------
